Approach 1: Cisco Systems Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow model takes estimates of a company’s future cash flows and discounts them back to today using a required rate of return, aiming to arrive at an intrinsic value per share.

For Cisco Systems, the model starts with last twelve months free cash flow of about US$12.4b. Analysts provide explicit forecasts for several years, and Simply Wall St then extends those projections further out using a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity approach. By 2030, projected free cash flow is US$19.8b, with intermediate years stepping up from around US$13.6b in 2026 to the low US$20b range by 2035 on an extrapolated basis.

After discounting those projected cash flows back to today, the DCF model points to an estimated intrinsic value of about US$86.16 per share. Against the recent share price of US$89.01, this implies the stock is roughly 3.3% overvalued, which is a small gap and well within the sort of margin of error that is common for this type of model.

data.title: Molina Reworks Product Mix With MAPD Exit And Medicaid Focus
data.description:
• Molina Healthcare (NYSE:MOH) plans to exit the Medicare Advantage Prescription Drug business starting in 2027.
  • The company is reducing its Marketplace exposure as part of a broader reshaping of its product portfolio.
  • Molina is ramping up its Florida CMS Kids contract, which has different profitability dynamics compared with Medicare and Marketplace plans.

Molina Healthcare operates as a managed care company with a focus on government sponsored health programs, and these moves shift emphasis away from Medicare Advantage and Marketplace products. Recent sector developments have highlighted regulatory scrutiny and margin pressure in these lines of business, which can affect how companies balance growth and risk. For investors, the refocus changes how Molina is positioned across Medicaid, Marketplace, Medicare and specialty contracts.

Looking ahead, the exit from MAPD in 2027 and the pullback in Marketplace exposure alter Molina's mix of revenue sources and potential earnings drivers. The expansion of the Florida CMS Kids contract adds a different source of membership and profit mechanics that could influence long term returns and volatility in results. Readers tracking NYSE:MOH may want to monitor how these changes affect capital allocation, pricing decisions and contract renewals over the next few years.

Stay updated on the most important news stories for Molina Healthcare by adding it to your watchlist or portfolio. Alternatively, explore our Community to discover new perspectives on Molina Healthcare.

2 things going right for Molina Healthcare that this headline doesn't cover.

The exit from the Medicare Advantage Prescription Drug product and lower exposure to Marketplace plans looks largely like a business mix clean up after a period of margin strain. Q1 2026 GAAP net income of US$14m on US$10.8b of revenue, plus the US$93m impairment tied to the MAPD exit, underlines how sensitive earnings have been to product level profitability. By reallocating focus toward Medicaid and integrated Medicare Duals, and ramping the Florida CMS Kids contract even with a potentially high initial medical loss ratio, Molina is leaning into segments that management appears more comfortable managing for medical cost trend and pricing. For you, the key question is whether this shift can make the reaffirmed 2026 guidance of about US$42b in premium revenue and at least US$5 of adjusted EPS less volatile than the swings seen through 2025.

Advertisement

How This Fits Into The Molina Healthcare Narrative

  • The move away from MAPD and some Marketplace exposure is consistent with the narrative focus on disciplined medical cost management and tighter alignment between rates and medical trends in core Medicaid and Duals contracts.
  • The US$93m MAPD impairment and Q1 margin compression also highlight the narrative risk that product level execution issues and higher medical costs can pressure earnings stability, even when revenue is near guidance.
  • The Florida CMS Kids ramp and any contract specific medical cost trends are only partly reflected in the narrative, so the ultimate profitability of these newer contracts could shift the story if early loss ratios run above expectations for longer.

Knowing what a company is worth starts with understanding its story. Check out one of the top narratives in the Simply Wall St Community for Molina Healthcare to help decide what it's worth to you.

The Risks and Rewards Investors Should Consider

  • ⚠️ Thin GAAP profitability in Q1 2026, with US$14m of net income on US$10.8b of revenue and a MAPD related impairment, shows how quickly earnings can compress when medical costs or product level assumptions move against the company.
  • ⚠️ Analysts have flagged 2 key risks, including the impact of large one off items on reported results and pressure on profit margins compared with the prior year, which may keep attention on execution versus guidance.
  • 🎁 Analysts have also identified 2 key rewards, including the view that the shares trade well below some fair value estimates, which may appeal to investors who are comfortable with the current risk profile.
  • 🎁 The refocus toward Medicaid and integrated Medicare Duals, alongside contracts like Florida CMS Kids, may support a business mix that management believes it can price and manage more consistently than the MAPD and Marketplace lines.

What To Watch Going Forward

From here, keep an eye on how quickly the MAPD exit costs drop out of the income statement, and whether adjusted EPS stays in line with the reaffirmed 2026 guidance. Trends in Medicaid membership attrition and rate updates will matter for how revenue around US$42b converts into earnings, especially against medical cost trend assumptions. It is also worth watching how the profitability of contracts such as Florida CMS Kids evolves, and how Molina’s choices compare with peers like UnitedHealth, Centene and Elevance Health that also balance Medicaid, Medicare and Marketplace exposure. All of these factors will shape whether this product mix reset delivers more predictable margins than investors saw through 2025.
